fluitenberg.e:

foxel.e: First attested as Fokselwijk in 1936. Derived from a contraction of vossenhol (“foxhole”).

foxwolde.e:

felland.e: First attested as Felland in 1867. Derived from Old Frisian fenland (“pasture”).

foxhol.e:

froombosch.e:

foxham.e:
First attested as van den vosham in 1488. Compound of Old Frisian foks (“fox”) and ham (“alluvial land in the bend of a river”).

foxholsterbosch.e:
First attested as Foxholsterbosch in 1877. Compound of the toponym Foxhol and bos (“rising forest”).

finsterwolde.e:
First attested as fynserwald in 1319. Likely a compound of Old Frisian winster (“left, northern”) and wold (“swamp forest, marsh forest”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Finnewôld.

finsterwolderhamrik.e:
First attested as Finsterwolderhamrik in 1877. Compound of the toponym Finsterwolde and hamrik (“village area, polder”).

farmsum.e:
First attested as fretmarashem in the 10th or 11th century. Compound of Old Frisian Fretmar (a personal name) and heem (“home, settlement”).

fiemel.e: First attested as fimilon in the 10th century. Derived from the dative plural form of an alternative version of the hydronym Fivel.

fraamklap.e:
First attested as Fraemtil in 1654. Compound of the toponym Fraam (a former castle) and klap (“drawbridge”).

fivelzigt.e: Compound of the hydronym Fivel and zicht (“view”).

fransum.e:

frytum.e:
First attested as frytemahuis in 1453. Derived from Old Frisian Fritema (a surname). Named after a former castle.

ferwerd.e: First attested as fatruwerde between 819 and 825. Borrowed from West Frisian Ferwert, derived in turn from a compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian werth (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”). Compare Feerwerd and Fedderwarden.

foudgum.e: Potentially attested as fotdenheim [read: foldenheim] in 944, attested with certainty as foldeghum in 1422. Borrowed from West Frisian Foudgum, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Foldo (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

feerwerd.e:
First attested as federwrt and federfurt around 820. Derived from a compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian wrt (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”). The first element may be Old Frisian Feder (a personal name). Compare Ferwerd and Fedderwarden.

friens.e: First attested as frenigge in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Friens, derived in turn from Old Frisian Frana (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi-.

finkum.e: First attested as finckum in 1335. Derived from a compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian hēm (“home, settlement”). folgeren.e: First attested as folgelen in 1543. Calque of West Frisian De Folgeren, derived from the plural form of folger (“newly reclaimed land lying next to old reclaimed land, isolated, low-quality land”). Compare De Volgerlanden.

frieschepalen.e: First attested as Vriesche Palen in 1622. Calque of West Frisian Fryske Peallen, derived from Frysk (“Frisian”) and the plural form of peal (“border, borderland”). The village is located on the border of the province.

fochteloo.e: First attested as fyochtelo in 1408. Potentially derived from a compound of a term cognate to vocht (“moisture”) and lo (“light forest”).

finkeburen.e: First attested as Finkeburen in 1851-1855. Calque of West Frisian Finkebuorren, a compound of either Finke (a personal name), vinke (“poor-quality turf”) or fink (“common chaffinch, Fringilla coelebs”) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

ferwoude.e: First attested as forwalda in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Ferwâlde, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian for (“forward, front”) and wald (“marsh forest, swamp forest”).

folsgare.e: First attested as ffoldesgare in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Folsgeare, potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian Foldo (a personal name) and gâra (“protruding piece of land”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

flansum.e: First attested as flansem in 1466. Borrowed from West Frisian Flânsum, potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian Flanso (a personal name) or Flandso and hēm (“home, settlement”).

feyteburen.e: Calque of West Frisian Feytebuorren, a compound of Feite (a personal name) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

franjumerburen.e: Potentially attested as frangilande in 944, attested with certainty as franghum in 1471. formerum.e:
